docs.title: stoke
docs.text:
Build, run, and scaffold projects in multiple languages.

stoke unifies development workflows for Python, Java, C, and C++ projects. One command-line interface for building, running, watching, and scaffolding — including popular frameworks like Spring Boot, FastAPI, Flask, and Django. Configure your project once in stoke.toml, and stoke handles compilation, dependency management, IDE integration, and more.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Multi-language: Build Python, Java, C, and C++ projects with the same commands
  • Language installation: Install Python/JDK/gcc via stoke install
  • Framework scaffolding: Spring Boot, FastAPI, Flask, Django
  • Python environments: venv or conda
  • Fast: Incremental compilation with header dependency tracking
  • Simple: One stoke.toml for the whole project
  • Watch mode: Auto-rebuild on file changes
  • Hot-reload: Restart processes on rebuild
  • IDE integration: Auto-generates VSCode/Eclipse/IntelliJ configs
  • Build profiles: Debug/Release/custom profiles
  • Dependency management: pip for Python, Maven for Java, vcpkg for C/C++

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
Create stoke.toml:

[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "cpp"
sources = ["src/**/*.cpp"]

Build and run:

stoke build
stoke run

That's it.

docs.title: What is stoke?
docs.text: A build tool that unifies workflows for Python, Java, C, and C++. One stoke.toml per project, one set of commands for all languages.

docs.title: Why not use language-specific tools?
docs.text:
Language-specific tools (pip, Maven, Make, Cargo) are excellent. But when you work across multiple languages, remembering which command builds what, configuring each ecosystem separately, and coordinating IDE setups becomes friction.

stoke provides a consistent interface without replacing the underlying tools. Under the hood, stoke uses pip, javac, Maven, gcc/clang, and vcpkg — it just wraps them consistently.

docs.title: Is stoke a replacement for CMake / Meson / Make?
docs.text: Not really. stoke is more like a project runner than a build system. It's opinionated and works out of the box for common cases. For complex build scenarios (cross-compilation, code generation, custom build steps), you probably need CMake.

docs.title: What languages does stoke support?
docs.text:
Currently:

  • Python
  • Java (JDK 17+)
  • C
  • C++

More languages may come in the future.

docs.title: Which platforms?
docs.text:
• Windows (native installer)
  • Linux (via pip)
  • macOS (via pip)

Actively developed on Windows (MSYS2/MinGW64).

docs.title: How do I update stoke?
docs.text:
Windows installer users: download the new installer and run it. It replaces the old version.

pip users: pip install -U stoke-build.

docs.title: How do I uninstall stoke?
docs.text:
Windows installer: use Windows "Add or Remove Programs" or run the uninstaller in the install directory.

pip: pip uninstall stoke-build.

docs.title: Can I have multiple targets in one project?
docs.text:
Yes. Any number of [targets.<name>] sections:

[targets.server]
language = "python"
entry = "server/main.py"

[targets.client]
language = "cpp"
sources = ["client/**/*.cpp"]

Build all: stoke build Build one: stoke build server

docs.title: Can targets use different languages?
docs.text: Yes. Each target has its own language.

docs.title: Can I share code between targets?
docs.text:
Yes. Adjust sources glob patterns to include shared code:

[targets.server]
language = "python"
sources = ["server/**/*.py", "shared/**/*.py"]

[targets.worker]
language = "python"
sources = ["worker/**/*.py", "shared/**/*.py"]

docs.title: Where's my build output?
docs.text: .stoke/{language}/{target}/{profile}/. See stoke build.

docs.title: Can I add custom build steps?
docs.text: Not directly. stoke doesn't support pre/post-build hooks yet. Wrap stoke build in a shell script if needed.

docs.title: Python: does stoke replace pip / venv?
docs.text: No. stoke uses venv and pip under the hood. It automates the venv creation, dependency installation, and PYTHONPATH setup.

docs.title: Java: does stoke use Maven?
docs.text:
stoke uses Maven Central for dependency downloads. It doesn't use Maven's build system — it invokes javac directly.

The generated pom.xml is for IDE integration only.

docs.title: C/C++: what compiler does stoke use?
docs.text:
• Linux default: gcc
  • macOS default: clang
  • Windows default: gcc (from MSYS2/MinGW)

Override with build profiles. MSVC (cl.exe) is not currently supported.

docs.title: C/C++: can I use CMake?
docs.text: Not through stoke. stoke has its own simple build model. If you need CMake, use CMake directly.

docs.title: Why is my build not using the cache?
docs.text:
Common reasons:

  • Header changed (C/C++ tracks headers automatically)
  • Source file's timestamp changed
  • Compile flags changed (different profile)
  • --force was used

Verbose mode may help debug:

stoke build -v

docs.title: Why does stoke build regenerate IDE files every time?
docs.text:
It doesn't — since v0.7.2. IDE files are only rewritten when their content changes.

If you see IDE files updated: X, Y, only those actually changed.

docs.title: Why is Lock file saved showing every time?
docs.text: Fixed in v0.7.2. Update to the latest version.

docs.title: Does stoke work in CI?
docs.text:
Yes. stoke build in a container or CI runner works the same as locally. Cache the .stoke/ directory to speed up subsequent builds.

Recommended: use lock_mode = "strict" in CI for reproducible builds.

docs.title: Does stoke work with Docker?
docs.text:
Yes. Install stoke in the Dockerfile:

RUN pip install stoke-build

Then stoke build normally.

docs.title: Can I use stoke as a subprocess from another tool?
docs.text:
Yes. Exit codes are meaningful:

  • 0: success
  • non-zero: failure

Output goes to stdout/stderr in the standard way.

docs.title: How can I contribute?
docs.text:
• Report bugs or request features: github.com/dvdsvds/stoke/issues
  • Pull requests welcome on GitHub

docs.title: Where's the source code?
docs.text:
github.com/dvdsvds/stoke

MIT license.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and solutions.

docs.title: stoke.toml not found
docs.text:
Cause: You ran stoke from outside a project directory.

Fix: cd into the project root (the directory containing stoke.toml).

docs.title: target 'X' not found in stoke.toml
docs.text:
Cause: The specified target doesn't exist.

Fix: List valid targets by opening stoke.toml. Available targets are shown in the error output.

docs.title: profile 'X' not found
docs.text:
Cause: Custom profile doesn't exist in stoke.toml.

Fix: Check [profiles.*] sections in stoke.toml. Only debug, release, and custom profiles you define are valid.

docs.title: Python X.Y not found
docs.text:
Cause: No Python matching python_version is installed.

Fix:

  1. Check detected Pythons: stoke python list
  2. Install the required Python version, or
  3. Change python_version in stoke.toml to a version you have

docs.title: JDK X not found
docs.text:
Cause: No JDK with the required major version.

Fix:

  1. Check detected JDKs: stoke java list
  2. Install a matching JDK (Adoptium recommended: adoptium.net)
  3. Set JAVA_HOME if needed
  4. Or change java_version in stoke.toml

docs.title: No C compiler detected
docs.text:
Cause: gcc/clang not in PATH.

Fix:

  • Windows: Install MSYS2 and run pacman -S mingw-w64-ucrt-x86_64-gcc. Add MSYS2 bin to PATH.
  • macOS: Install Xcode Command Line Tools: xcode-select --install
  • Linux: sudo apt install build-essential (Ubuntu/Debian) or equivalent

docs.title: stoke run fails silently
docs.text:
Cause: The target hasn't been built yet.

Fix: stoke build first.

docs.title: Python: ModuleNotFoundError
docs.text:
Cause: Import paths not set up correctly.

Fix:

  • Ensure each folder has an __init__.py
  • Use full paths from src/: from utils.helpers import ...
  • Check Python source layout

docs.title: Java: Could not find or load main class
docs.text:
Cause: main_class in stoke.toml doesn't match the actual class name.

Fix: Verify the fully qualified class name matches. Example:

// src/main/java/com/example/Main.java
package com.example;
public class Main { ... }

main_class = "com.example.Main"    # fully qualified

docs.title: Stale builds after external changes
docs.text:
Cause: You changed something outside sources (e.g. moved files, changed env vars) and cache is stale.

Fix: Force rebuild:

stoke build --force

docs.title: Lock file mismatch
docs.text:
Cause: Lock file references tools/deps that no longer exist.

Fix: Full reset:

stoke clean --all
stoke build

docs.title: Library install takes forever
docs.text:
Cause: vcpkg builds from source. Some libraries (Boost, Qt) take a very long time.

Fix: Be patient. First install is slow; subsequent uses are fast.

docs.title: Library not found after install
docs.text:
Cause: vcpkg installed the library but stoke's cache is stale.

Fix:

stoke build --force

docs.title: vcpkg corrupted
docs.text:
Cause: Interrupted install or system issue.

Fix:

stoke uninstall vcpkg
stoke install vcpkg
stoke build --force

docs.title: VSCode showing wrong Python interpreter
docs.text:
Cause: VSCode Python extension picked a global Python instead of the venv.

Fix:

  1. stoke build to regenerate .vscode/settings.json
  2. Ctrl+Shift+P → "Python: Select Interpreter" → pick .stoke/python/<target>/venv/

docs.title: VSCode lag on some systems
docs.text:
Cause: File watcher or extensions overloading.

Fix:

  1. Confirm .vscode/settings.json excludes .stoke/
  2. Disable hardware acceleration: settings.json → "disable-hardware-acceleration": true
  3. Disable unused extensions
  4. Restart VSCode

Tracking issue: VSCode lag on some systems

docs.title: C++ IntelliSense not working
docs.text:
Cause: compile_commands.json missing or IDE not picking it up.

Fix:

  1. Run stoke build to generate compile_commands.json
  2. Ensure your extension reads it (Microsoft C/C++ extension reads .vscode/c_cpp_properties.json; clangd reads compile_commands.json)

docs.title: Changes not detected
docs.text:
Cause: Files are outside the sources glob patterns.

Fix: Check sources in stoke.toml covers all your source files.

docs.title: hot-reload doesn't restart
docs.text:
Cause: Process didn't exit cleanly, or output buffered.

Fix:

  • Try again (transient issues)
  • For Python/Java, ensure your program handles SIGTERM/SIGINT properly

docs.title: Still stuck?
docs.text:
• Search or open an issue: github.com/dvdsvds/stoke/issues
  • Check the FAQ

docs.title: IDE Integration
docs.text: stoke generates IDE configuration files automatically during stoke build. Open your project in the IDE — everything just works.

docs.title: Supported IDEs
docs.text: IDE Python Java C/C++ VSCode ✓ ✓ ✓ Eclipse ✓ IntelliJ / JetBrains ✓ (via Maven) clangd-based ✓

docs.title: Files generated
docs.text:
Per-project (in project root):

  • .vscode/settings.json

For C/C++: - .vscode/c_cpp_properties.json (Microsoft C/C++ extension) - compile_commands.json (clangd, Native Debug)

For Java: - .classpath (Eclipse format, used by VSCode Java extension) - .project (Eclipse format) - pom.xml (Maven format)

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
settings.json includes:

  • Python: interpreter path, extra paths, exclusions
  • Java: source paths, referenced JARs
  • C/C++: include paths, defines, compiler path

Also excludes .stoke/ from file watching to reduce editor lag.

docs.title: Extensions
docs.text:
Recommended VSCode extensions:

  • Python — Microsoft Python extension
  • Java — Extension Pack for Java
  • C/C++ — Microsoft C/C++ extension OR clangd

docs.title: Eclipse (Java)
docs.text:
Import as an existing Eclipse project:

  1. File → Import → General → Existing Projects into Workspace
  2. Select the project folder

Eclipse reads .classpath and .project.

docs.title: IntelliJ / JetBrains (Java)
docs.text:
Import as a Maven project:

  1. File → Open → select the project folder or pom.xml
  2. IntelliJ reads pom.xml

docs.title: clangd (C/C++)
docs.text:
compile_commands.json at the project root is the standard for clangd-based tools:

  • clangd LSP server
  • CLion (via clangd)
  • Neovim + clangd
  • Emacs + clangd
  • Any editor with clangd support

No extra config needed. Just open the project.

docs.title: Multi-project workspaces
docs.text:
For a repository with multiple stoke projects: myworkspace/ ├── frontend/ # python │ └── stoke.toml ├── backend/ # java │ └── stoke.toml └── engine/ # cpp └── stoke.toml

Run in the root:

stoke ide-sync

Generates:

  • .vscode/settings.json — workspace-level settings
  • myworkspace.code-workspace — multi-root VSCode workspace

Open the .code-workspace file to work on all projects together.

See stoke ide-sync for details.

docs.title: Regenerating IDE files
docs.text:
IDE files are regenerated on every stoke build. If they get out of sync (e.g. after changing dependencies), just rebuild:

stoke build

Or force it:

stoke build --force

docs.title: Not modifying manually
docs.text:
Since IDE files are regenerated, manual edits are overwritten on the next build. Configure via stoke.toml instead.

Add extra include paths in stoke.toml:

[targets.myapp]
include_dirs = ["third_party/include"]

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• stoke ide-sync
  • stoke build

docs.title: vcpkg Integration
docs.text: stoke integrates with vcpkg for C/C++ dependency management.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install vcpkg:

stoke install vcpkg

This clones vcpkg into a stoke-managed location and bootstraps it. No admin privileges needed.

Check installation:

stoke vcpkg version

Uninstall:

stoke uninstall vcpkg

docs.title: Installing libraries
docs.text: Two ways:

docs.title: Method 1: Direct install command
docs.text:
stoke vcpkg install fmt
stoke vcpkg install sqlite3

This:

  1. Downloads and builds the library via vcpkg
  2. Adds it to stoke.toml under [targets.<name>.deps]

By default, the library is added to the first target. Specify a target:

stoke vcpkg install fmt --target=engine

docs.title: Method 2: Add to stoke.toml manually
docs.text:
[targets.myapp.deps]
fmt = "*"
sqlite3 = "*"

Then run:

stoke build

stoke automatically installs any declared but missing libraries.

docs.title: Removing libraries
docs.text:
stoke vcpkg remove fmt

This:

  1. Removes from stoke.toml
  2. Runs vcpkg's remove command

docs.title: Listing installed libraries
docs.text:
stoke vcpkg list

Or for a specific target:

stoke vcpkg list --target=engine

docs.title: Specific versions
docs.text:
Install a specific version:

stoke vcpkg install fmt --version=10.1.1

Or in stoke.toml:

[targets.myapp.deps]
fmt = "10.1.1"

Use "*" for the latest available:

[targets.myapp.deps]
fmt = "*"

docs.title: How stoke uses vcpkg
docs.text:
At build time:

  1. stoke reads [targets.<name>.deps]
  2. Verifies each library is installed via vcpkg
  3. Adds vcpkg include paths (e.g. installed/x64-mingw-dynamic/include/) to compile flags
  4. Links vcpkg library files (.a, .lib, .so, etc.) at link time

Include paths and library paths are automatic. In your code:

#include <fmt/format.h>          // works, no extra config needed

docs.title: Language compatibility
docs.text:
Some libraries are C-only, some are C++-only.

C project:

[targets.myapp]
language = "c"

[targets.myapp.deps]
sqlite3 = "*"    # ✓ C library
fmt = "*"        # ✗ C++ only, stoke will error

C++ project:

[targets.myapp]
language = "cpp"

[targets.myapp.deps]
sqlite3 = "*"    # ✓ works (C libraries usable from C++)
fmt = "*"        # ✓ C++ library

stoke validates compatibility before installing.

docs.title: Triplets
docs.text:
vcpkg builds libraries per triplet (architecture + platform + linkage).

Auto-detected default:

  • Windows MinGW64: x64-mingw-dynamic
  • Windows MSVC: x64-windows
  • Linux x64: x64-linux
  • macOS: x64-osx or arm64-osx

Stored in .stoke/lock.toml per dependency.

docs.title: Library not found after install
docs.text: Try stoke build --force to clear the cache and re-scan vcpkg.

docs.title: Long install times
docs.text: First install of some libraries (like Boost, Qt) can take an hour. This is vcpkg building from source. Subsequent builds are fast.

docs.title: Reset vcpkg
docs.text:
If vcpkg gets corrupted:

stoke uninstall vcpkg
stoke install vcpkg
stoke build --force

docs.text:

docs.title: stoke build
docs.text: Build a target.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
stoke build [target] [options]

If target is omitted, the first target in stoke.toml is used.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --force Ignore cache and rebuild everything --debug Build with the debug profile (default) --release Build with the release profile --profile <name> Build with a custom profile from stoke.toml -v, --verbose Show detailed build output

docs.title: Basic build
docs.text:
stoke build

Builds the default target with the debug profile.

docs.title: Specify target
docs.text:
stoke build myapp

Builds the myapp target.

docs.title: Release build
docs.text:
stoke build --release

Optimized build (-O2 for C/C++, defines NDEBUG).

docs.title: Custom profile
docs.text:
# stoke.toml
[profiles.small]
compile_flags = ["-Os", "-flto"]
defines = { NDEBUG = 1 }

stoke build --profile=small

docs.title: Force rebuild
docs.text:
stoke build --force

Ignores the cache and recompiles everything. Useful when you suspect stale artifacts.

docs.title: Verbose output
docs.text:
stoke build -v

Shows compiler paths, per-file compilation status, and detailed steps.

docs.title: Output structure
docs.text:
Build artifacts are organized by language, target, and profile: .stoke/ ├── {language}/ │ └── {target}/ │ └── {profile}/ │ ├── objects/ # C/C++ .o files │ ├── classes/ # Java .class files │ └── {target}.exe # Final executable ├── cache.json # Incremental build cache └── lock.toml # Locked versions

Example for a C project: .stoke/ ├── c/ │ └── myapp/ │ ├── debug/ │ │ ├── objects/ │ │ └── myapp.exe │ └── release/ │ ├── objects/ │ └── myapp.exe ├── cache.json └── lock.toml

docs.title: Incremental compilation
docs.text:
stoke tracks file modifications and header dependencies. Only changed files are recompiled.

For C/C++, header dependencies are tracked via gcc's -MMD flag. If header.h changes, all files that #include "header.h" (directly or indirectly) will be recompiled.

To bypass the cache, use --force.

docs.title: Build profiles
docs.text:
Default profiles:

  • debug — -O0 -g -Wall, defines DEBUG (C/C++ only)
  • release — -O2, defines NDEBUG (C/C++ only)

Note: profiles only affect C/C++ builds. Python and Java ignore profiles.

Custom profiles are defined in stoke.toml:

[profiles.myprofile]
compile_flags = ["-O3", "-march=native"]
defines = { MYFLAG = 1 }
compiler = "clang"  # optional: override compiler

See Profiles for details.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• stoke run — run the built target
  • stoke watch — auto-rebuild on changes
  • stoke clean — remove build artifacts
  • Profiles — profile system reference

docs.title: stoke clean
docs.text: Delete build artifacts.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
stoke clean [target] [options]

If target is omitted, all targets are cleaned.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --all Also delete the lock file (full reset)

docs.title: Clean all targets
docs.text:
stoke clean

Removes:

  • .stoke/{language}/{target}/ for each target
  • .stoke/cache.json
  • All __pycache__ folders in the project

docs.title: Clean a specific target
docs.text:
stoke clean myapp

Only removes artifacts for myapp.

docs.title: Full reset
docs.text:
stoke clean --all

Also deletes .stoke/lock.toml, forcing dependency re-resolution on the next build.

Use this if: - Lock file is corrupted - You want to pull the latest versions of dependencies - Switching Python/JDK versions caused issues

docs.title: What gets deleted
docs.text:
Item clean clean --all Object files (.o, .class) ✓ ✓ Executables ✓ ✓ Python venv ✓ ✓ .stoke/cache.json ✓ ✓ __pycache__/ ✓ ✓ .stoke/lock.toml ✓ IDE files (.vscode/, .classpath, etc.)

IDE files are never deleted; they're regenerated on the next build.

docs.title: After clean
docs.text:
Next build will:

  • Recompile everything (no cache)
  • Recreate the Python venv (if applicable)
  • Reinstall dependencies (with --all)

docs.title: Related
docs.text: • stoke build --force — force rebuild without deleting artifacts

docs.title: stoke hot-reload
docs.text: Watch for file changes, rebuild, and restart the running process.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: stoke hot-reload [target] [options]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --debug Debug build (default) --release Release build --profile <name> Custom profile -v, --verbose Show detailed build output

docs.title: Example
docs.text: stoke hot-reload

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
[hot-reload] Rebuilding 'myapp'... Using c compiler 15.2.0 Compiled 1 file(s) Build complete: myapp [hot-reload] Starting: .stoke\cpp\myapp\debug\myapp.exe [hot-reload] Watching: C:\myproject\src [hot-reload] Press Ctrl+C to stop. Hello from stoke!

When you save a file, stoke stops the process, rebuilds, and restarts: [hot-reload] Detected changes in: src\main.c [hot-reload] Stopping process... ================================================== [hot-reload] Rebuilding 'myapp'... Using c compiler 15.2.0 Compiled 1 file(s) Build complete: myapp [hot-reload] Starting: .stoke\cpp\myapp\debug\myapp.exe Hello from stoke!

Press Ctrl+C to stop the watcher and the process.

docs.title: When to use it
docs.text:
Best for long-running processes:

  • Web servers (Flask, Spring Boot, etc.)
  • Development scripts with state
  • Background workers

For batch programs that finish and exit quickly, stoke watch is usually enough.

docs.title: Language-specific behavior
docs.text:
Same as stoke run:

  • Python: runs the entry script
  • Java: runs main_class
  • C/C++: runs the compiled executable

docs.title: C/C++ note
docs.text:
For C/C++, stoke stops the process before rebuilding. This is necessary because the linker can't overwrite a running executable on most platforms.

For Python and Java, stoke rebuilds first and stops the process after, since there's no such limitation.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• stoke watch — rebuild only, don't restart
  • stoke run — single run

docs.title: stoke ide-sync
docs.text: Generate IDE configurations for a multi-project workspace.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Run from a directory that contains multiple stoke projects:

stoke ide-sync

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
Scans subdirectories for stoke.toml files and generates:

  • .vscode/settings.json — VSCode workspace settings
  • {workspace_name}.code-workspace — VSCode multi-root workspace file

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Directory structure: myworkspace/ ├── frontend/ │ └── stoke.toml # python ├── backend/ │ └── stoke.toml # java └── engine/ └── stoke.toml # cpp

Run: $ cd myworkspace $ stoke ide-sync Scanning for stoke projects under C:\Users...\myworkspace... Found 3 project(s): [python] frontend [java] backend [cpp] engine Generated: C:\Users...\myworkspace.vscode\settings.json Generated: C:\Users...\myworkspace\myworkspace.code-workspace Open in VSCode: C:\Users...\myworkspace\myworkspace.code-workspace

Open the .code-workspace file in VSCode to work on all projects together with proper language support for each.

docs.title: Individual project IDE files
docs.text:
stoke build also generates per-project IDE files (VSCode settings, Eclipse .classpath, pom.xml, etc.). Those are separate from ide-sync.

  • stoke build — per-project IDE files (single project)
  • stoke ide-sync — workspace-level VSCode setup (multiple projects)

docs.title: Related
docs.text: • IDE Integration — deep dive

docs.title: stoke init
docs.text: Create a new stoke project interactively.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
stoke init

Runs an interactive wizard in the current directory.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
The wizard asks:

  1. Project name — defaults to the current folder name
  2. Language — Python, Java, C, or C++
  3. Language version — Python 3.12, Java 25, C11, C++20, etc.
  4. Entry point / main class — depending on the language
  5. Dependencies — optional

Then it generates:

  • stoke.toml — project configuration
  • src/ — source directory
  • A hello-world source file

docs.title: Example: C++ project
docs.text:
$ mkdir myapp && cd myapp $ stoke init Project name [myapp]: Language [python/java/c/cpp]: cpp C++ standard [17/20/23] [20]: Executable name [myapp]: Install vcpkg for C/C++ library management? [y/N]: n Created: stoke.toml src/main.cpp Try: stoke build stoke run

Generated stoke.toml:

[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "cpp"
sources = ["src/**/*.cpp"]
cpp_standard = "c++20"

docs.title: Example: Python project
docs.text:
$ mkdir myapp && cd myapp $ stoke init Project name [myapp]: Language [python/java/c/cpp]: python Python version [3.10/3.11/3.12/3.13]: 3.12 Entry file [src/main.py]: Created: stoke.toml src/main.py

Generated stoke.toml:

[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "python"
python_version = "3.12"
entry = "src/main.py"
sources = ["src/**/*.py"]

docs.title: Example: Java project
docs.text:
$ mkdir myapp && cd myapp $ stoke init Project name [myapp]: Language [python/java/c/cpp]: java Java version [17/21/25]: 25 Package [com.myapp]: Main class [Main]: Created: stoke.toml src/main/java/com/myapp/Main.java

Generated stoke.toml:

[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "java"
java_version = "25"
sources = ["src/main/java/**/*.java"]
main_class = "com.myapp.Main"

docs.title: After init
docs.text:
Build and run:

stoke build
stoke run

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Quick Start
  • stoke.toml reference

docs.title: Commands Overview
docs.text: stoke commands are organized by purpose.

docs.title: Build & run
docs.text: Command Description stoke build Compile the target stoke run Run the built target stoke watch Auto-rebuild on file changes stoke hot-reload Rebuild + restart process

docs.title: Project management
docs.text: Command Description stoke init Create a new project stoke clean Delete build artifacts stoke ide-sync Generate IDE configs

docs.title: Toolchain
docs.text: Command Description stoke python list List detected Python installations stoke java list List detected JDKs stoke c list List detected C compilers stoke cpp list List detected C++ compilers

docs.title: C/C++ libraries
docs.text:
Command Description stoke install vcpkg Install vcpkg stoke uninstall vcpkg Uninstall vcpkg stoke vcpkg install <lib> Install a library stoke vcpkg remove <lib> Remove a library stoke vcpkg list List installed libraries stoke vcpkg version Show vcpkg version

See vcpkg guide for details.

docs.title: Global options
docs.text: Available across most commands:

docs.title: --verbose (-v)
docs.text:
Show detailed output:

stoke build --verbose

Default output is concise. Verbose shows compiler paths, dependency scanning, and per-file progress.

docs.title: Language selection
docs.text:
Set STOKE_LANG=ko in your environment to see help messages in Korean:

STOKE_LANG=ko stoke --help

Default is English. CLI output (build progress, errors) remains in English.

docs.title: Getting help
docs.text:
Each command has its own help:

stoke --help
stoke build --help
stoke vcpkg install --help

docs.title: stoke run
docs.text: Run the built target.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
stoke run [target] [options]

If target is omitted, the first target in stoke.toml is used.

stoke run does not rebuild. It runs the previously built artifact. Run stoke build first if you've made changes.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --debug Run the debug build (default) --release Run the release build --profile <name> Run a specific custom profile build

docs.title: Basic run
docs.text:
stoke run

Runs the default target with the debug profile.

docs.title: Specify target
docs.text: stoke run myapp

docs.title: Run release build
docs.text:
stoke build --release
stoke run --release

docs.title: Run custom profile
docs.text:
stoke build --profile=small
stoke run --profile=small

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
Runs the entry script with the project's venv:

[targets.myapp]
language = "python"
entry = "src/main.py"
$ stoke run Running: C:\myproject\src\main.py Hello from stoke!

docs.title: Java
docs.text:
Runs the class specified by main_class:

[targets.myapp]
language = "java"
main_class = "com.example.Main"
$ stoke run Running: com.example.Main Hello from stoke!

The classpath includes compiled .class files and Maven dependencies.

docs.title: C / C++
docs.text: Runs the compiled executable: $ stoke run Running: .stoke\cpp\myapp\debug\myapp.exe Hello from stoke!

docs.title: Exit codes
docs.text: The exit code of stoke run matches the target's exit code. If your program returns non-zero, stoke run also returns non-zero.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• stoke build — build before running
  • stoke hot-reload — rebuild and restart on file changes

docs.title: stoke watch
docs.text: Watch for file changes and rebuild automatically.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: stoke watch [target] [options]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --debug Debug build (default) --release Release build --profile <name> Custom profile -v, --verbose Show detailed build output

docs.title: Example
docs.text: stoke watch

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
[watch] Rebuilding 'myapp'... Using c compiler 15.2.0 Compiled 1 file(s) Build complete: myapp [watch] Watching: C:\myproject\src [watch] Press Ctrl+C to stop.

When you save a file, stoke automatically rebuilds: [watch] Detected changes in: src\main.c ================================================== [watch] Rebuilding 'myapp'... Using c compiler 15.2.0 Compiled 1 file(s) Build complete: myapp

Press Ctrl+C to stop.

docs.title: What's watched
docs.text:
stoke watches the directories that contain your sources patterns. For example, with:

[targets.myapp]
sources = ["src/**/*.c"]

stoke watches src/.

Only files matching the source extensions for your language are considered:

  • Python: .py
  • Java: .java
  • C: .c, .h
  • C++: .cpp, .hpp, .h

Other file changes are ignored.

docs.title: Debouncing
docs.text: Multiple rapid changes are debounced. If you save 5 files in quick succession, stoke rebuilds once, not 5 times.

docs.title: Profiles work with watch
docs.text:
Watch respects the profile you choose:

stoke watch --release

Builds into .stoke/{lang}/{target}/release/ on every change.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• stoke hot-reload — watch + restart the process
  • stoke build — single build

docs.title: Lock File
docs.text: .stoke/lock.toml records the exact versions of tools and dependencies used in each build. It ensures reproducibility: another developer (or CI) building your project gets the same versions.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
• Reproducibility: same versions across machines
  • Speed: skip re-resolving dependencies if lock matches
  • Auditability: see exactly what versions your build uses

docs.title: Location
docs.text: myproject/ ├── stoke.toml └── .stoke/ └── lock.toml # ← lock file

docs.title: Should you commit it?
docs.text:
Yes for applications, no for libraries.

  • Application: commit lock.toml to git. This pins versions so all developers and CI use the same versions.
  • Library: don't commit. Let users of the library resolve fresh versions.

Add to .gitignore for libraries: .stoke/lock.toml

docs.title: Build Profiles
docs.text: Build profiles let you switch between different build configurations without editing your code. Currently supported for C and C++ only.

docs.title: Built-in profiles
docs.text: stoke provides two default profiles:

docs.title: debug
docs.text:
compile_flags = ["-O0", "-g", "-Wall"]
defines = { DEBUG = 1 }

Default when you run stoke build. Good for development:

  • No optimization (-O0)
  • Full debug info (-g)
  • All warnings enabled (-Wall)
  • DEBUG defined for conditional compilation

docs.title: release
docs.text:
compile_flags = ["-O2"]
defines = { NDEBUG = 1 }

Use with --release:

  • Optimization (-O2)
  • NDEBUG defined (disables assert(), etc.)

docs.title: Using profiles
docs.text:
stoke build                     # debug (default)
stoke build --debug             # debug (explicit)
stoke build --release           # release
stoke build --profile=    # custom profile

Corresponding stoke run and stoke watch:

stoke run --release
stoke watch --profile=custom
stoke hot-reload --release

docs.title: Custom profiles
docs.text:
Define custom profiles in stoke.toml:

[profiles.<name>]
compile_flags = [...]     # extra compiler flags
defines = { KEY = value } # preprocessor defines
compiler = "..."          # optional: override compiler

docs.title: Example: size-optimized
docs.text:
[profiles.small]
compile_flags = ["-Os", "-flto", "-s"]
defines = { NDEBUG = 1 }

stoke build --profile=small

docs.title: Example: native optimization
docs.text:
[profiles.native]
compile_flags = ["-O3", "-march=native"]
defines = { NDEBUG = 1, NATIVE_BUILD = 1 }

docs.title: Example: clang override
docs.text:
[profiles.clang]
compiler = "clang"
compile_flags = ["-O2", "-Wall", "-Wextra"]

The compiler field overrides the default compiler for this profile. Value: "gcc" or "clang".

docs.title: Output directories
docs.text:
Each profile builds into its own directory: .stoke/ └── cpp/ └── myapp/ ├── debug/ │ └── myapp.exe ├── release/ │ └── myapp.exe └── small/ └── myapp.exe

Rebuilding a different profile doesn't affect others. You can maintain multiple builds simultaneously.

docs.title: Conflicts
docs.text:
You can't combine flag styles:

stoke build --debug --release           # error: can't combine
stoke build --release --profile=small   # error: can't combine

docs.title: Not available for Python/Java
docs.text: Profiles apply to C/C++ only. Python and Java builds ignore the profile flag but still use profile-based output directories: .stoke/ ├── python/ │ └── myapp/ │ └── venv/ # same venv regardless of profile └── java/ └── myapp/ └── debug/ # default profile dir └── classes/

docs.title: Available flags
docs.text:
Profile fields:

Field Type Description compile_flags list[string] Extra flags for the compiler defines table Preprocessor -D defines compiler string Override compiler ("gcc" or "clang")

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• stoke build
  • Languages / C/C++

docs.title: stoke.toml Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for the stoke.toml configuration file.

docs.title: File structure
docs.text:
[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "cpp"
sources = ["src/**/*.cpp"]

[targets.myapp.deps]
fmt = "*"

[profiles.custom]
compile_flags = ["-O2"]

Sections:

  • [project] — project-wide metadata
  • [targets.<name>] — one section per build target
  • [targets.<name>.deps] — dependencies for a target
  • [profiles.<name>] — build profiles (C/C++ only)

docs.title: [project]
docs.text:
Project-level configuration.

[project]
name = "myapp"           # Required. Project name.
version = "0.1.0"        # Optional. Project version.
lock_mode = "auto"       # Optional. Lock file behavior.
jobs = 4                 # Optional. Default parallel build workers.

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Type Description name string Project name version string Semver-ish version lock_mode "auto" | "strict" | "off" Lock file behavior (default: "auto") jobs int Default parallel workers for build (C/C++)

docs.title: lock_mode values
docs.text:
• "auto": use lock file if present, update as needed
  • "strict": require lock file, fail if versions don't match
  • "off": don't use or write lock file

docs.title: [targets.<name>]
docs.text:
One section per build target. Multiple targets allowed:

[targets.server]
language = "python"
entry = "src/server.py"

[targets.worker]
language = "python"
entry = "src/worker.py"

Build a specific target:

stoke build server
stoke run worker

If no target is specified, the first one in the file is used.

docs.title: Common fields
docs.text: Field Required Description language Yes "python", "java", "c", "cpp" sources Depends Glob patterns for source files

docs.title: Python-specific fields
docs.text: Field Description python_version Required Python version entry Entry script path

docs.title: Java-specific fields
docs.text: Field Description java_version Required JDK major version main_class Fully qualified main class

docs.title: C/C++-specific fields
docs.text: Field Description c_standard C standard ("c11", "c17", etc.) cpp_standard C++ standard ("c++17", "c++20", etc.) include_dirs Extra include directories jobs Override project-level jobs

docs.title: [targets.<name>.deps]
docs.text: Dependencies for a specific target. Format varies by language.

docs.title: Python (pip)
docs.text:
[targets.myapp.deps]
requests = "*"
flask = ">=2.0"
numpy = "==1.24.3"
mypackage = "git+https://github.com/user/repo"

docs.title: Java (Maven)
docs.text:
[targets.myapp.deps]
"com.google.code.gson:gson" = "2.10.1"
"org.slf4j:slf4j-api" = "2.0.9"

Key format: "groupId:artifactId".

docs.title: C / C++ (vcpkg)
docs.text:
[targets.myapp.deps]
fmt = "*"
sqlite3 = "*"

Version is passed to vcpkg. "*" means latest available.

docs.title: [profiles.<name>]
docs.text:
Build profiles for C/C++.

[profiles.small]
compile_flags = ["-Os", "-flto"]
defines = { NDEBUG = 1 }

[profiles.clang]
compiler = "clang"

See Profiles for the full reference.

docs.title: Full example
docs.text:
Multi-target project:

[project]
name = "myservice"
version = "1.0.0"

# Python backend
[targets.backend]
language = "python"
python_version = "3.12"
entry = "backend/main.py"
sources = ["backend/**/*.py"]

[targets.backend.deps]
fastapi = "*"
uvicorn = "*"

# C++ engine
[targets.engine]
language = "cpp"
sources = ["engine/src/**/*.cpp"]
cpp_standard = "c++20"
include_dirs = ["engine/include"]
jobs = 8

[targets.engine.deps]
fmt = "*"
spdlog = "*"

# Custom profile
[profiles.native]
compile_flags = ["-O3", "-march=native"]

Build individual targets:

stoke build backend
stoke build engine --profile=native

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Profiles
  • Lock file
  • Language guides: Python, Java, C/C++

docs.title: Chi
docs.text:
Create a Chi project via:

stoke init chi

Chi is a lightweight, idiomatic router for Go, built on net/http.

docs.title: Prompts
docs.text:
• Project name: directory name for the project
  • Go module name: e.g. github.com/user/myapp (defaults to project name)

docs.title: Generated files
docs.text:
myapp/
├── stoke.toml
├── go.mod
├── main.go                    # Chi entry point
└── handlers/
    └── hello.go               # sample handlers

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: • github.com/go-chi/chi/v5 (installed via go get)

docs.title: Default settings
docs.text:
• Port: 8080
  • Middleware: Logger (built-in)
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → {"message": "Hello from Chi + stoke!"}
  • GET /hello/{name} → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

Open http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: Customization
docs.text:
• Change port: edit http.ListenAndServe(":8080", r) in main.go
  • Add handlers: create files in handlers/ and register in main.go
  • Add middleware: use r.Use(...) in main.go

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Chi follows Go's net/http conventions, so handlers use func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request). This makes it easy to use existing Go HTTP libraries and middleware.

docs.title: Django
docs.text:
Create a Django project via:

stoke init django

docs.title: Prompts
docs.text:
• Project name: directory name for the project (also used as Django project package)
  • Python version: selected from detected installations (same as stoke init)
  • Environment type: venv or conda

docs.title: Generated files
docs.text:
myapp/
├── stoke.toml
└── src/
    ├── main.py                        # stoke run entry (calls manage.py runserver)
    ├── manage.py                      # Django management CLI
    ├── myapp/                         # project package
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── settings.py                # Django settings
    │   ├── urls.py                    # root URL config
    │   ├── wsgi.py
    │   └── asgi.py
    ├── hello/                         # sample app
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── apps.py
    │   ├── views.py
    │   ├── urls.py
    │   └── templates/
    │       └── hello/
    │           └── index.html         # sample template
    └── static/
        └── style.css                  # sample stylesheet

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: • django (latest)

docs.title: Default settings
docs.text:
• Host: 127.0.0.1
  • Port: 8000
  • DEBUG: True
  • Database: SQLite (db.sqlite3)
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → HTML page (rendered from hello/templates/hello/index.html)
  • GET /api/hello/<name>/ → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}
  • GET /admin/ → Django admin

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

Open http://localhost:8000/

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• main.py runs manage.py runserver via subprocess, so stoke run starts the development server
  • 18 initial migrations are pending — run python src/manage.py migrate to apply
  • For production, use a proper WSGI/ASGI server (gunicorn, uvicorn)

docs.title: Customization
docs.text:
• Change port: pass argument to runserver in src/main.py
  • Add views: edit src/hello/views.py and register in src/hello/urls.py
  • Add apps: create new folders under src/, register in INSTALLED_APPS in settings.py
  • Add templates: create .html files in app's templates/<appname>/
  • Add static files: place in src/static/

├── main.go                    # Gin entry point
└── handlers/
    └── hello.go               # sample handlers

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: • github.com/gin-gonic/gin (installed via go get)

docs.title: Default settings
docs.text:
• Port: 8080
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → {"message": "Hello from Gin + stoke!"}
  • GET /hello/:name → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

Open http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: Customization
docs.text:
• Change port: edit r.Run(":8080") in main.go
  • Add handlers: create files in handlers/ and register them in main.go
  • Add middleware: use r.Use(...) in main.go

stoke init hono

docs.title: NestJS

myapp/

docs.title: Framework scaffolding
docs.text:
stoke generates ready-to-run projects for popular frameworks with a single command:

# Java
stoke init spring-boot

# Python
stoke init fastapi
stoke init flask
stoke init django

# Go
stoke init gin
stoke init echo
stoke init fiber
stoke init chi

# JavaScript
stoke init express
stoke init fastify

# TypeScript
stoke init nextjs
stoke init nestjs
stoke init vite
stoke init nuxt
stoke init sveltekit
stoke init hono

Each command prompts for basic settings and generates the project structure with sample code.

docs.title: Java
docs.text: Command Framework stoke init spring-boot Spring Boot (via Spring Initializr)

docs.title: Python
docs.text: Command Framework stoke init fastapi FastAPI + uvicorn stoke init flask Flask + Jinja2 templates stoke init django Django (full project + app)

docs.title: Go
docs.text: Command Framework stoke init gin Gin — popular, fast HTTP framework stoke init echo Echo — minimalist, high performance stoke init fiber Fiber — Express-style API stoke init chi Chi — lightweight router using stdlib

docs.title: JavaScript
docs.text: Command Framework stoke init express Express — classic Node.js web framework stoke init fastify Fastify — fast, low-overhead framework

docs.title: TypeScript
docs.text: Command Framework stoke init nextjs Next.js — React full-stack stoke init nestjs NestJS — Angular-style backend stoke init vite Vite — fast frontend build tool stoke init nuxt Nuxt — Vue full-stack stoke init sveltekit SvelteKit — Svelte full-stack stoke init hono Hono — edge computing framework

docs.title: What's generated
docs.text:
Each framework page below documents the details:

Java: - Spring Boot

Python: - FastAPI - Flask - Django

Go: - Gin - Echo - Fiber - Chi

JavaScript: - Express - Fastify

TypeScript: - Next.js - NestJS - Vite - Nuxt - SvelteKit - Hono

docs.title: After scaffolding
docs.text:
Python, Go, JavaScript frameworks (Express, Fastify) use the same workflow:

stoke build
stoke run

Spring Boot uses Maven:

mvnw spring-boot:run    # Linux/macOS
mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run  # Windows

TypeScript frameworks (Next.js, NestJS, Vite, Nuxt, SvelteKit, Hono) use each framework's own commands:

npm run dev     # Development
npm run build   # Production

stoke calls the official scaffolding tools (create-next-app, @nestjs/cli, nuxi, sv create, etc.) so you get the latest project templates.

docs.title: Spring Boot
docs.text:
Create a Spring Boot project via:

stoke init spring-boot

This uses Spring Initializr API to download a standard Spring Boot project.

docs.title: Prompts
docs.text:
• Project name: directory name for the project
  • Group ID: Maven groupId (e.g. com.example)
  • Spring Boot version: selected from available RELEASE versions (fetched from Spring Initializr)
  • Java version: 17, 21, or 25
  • Build tool: Maven or Gradle
  • Packaging: jar or war
  • Dependencies: comma-separated (e.g. web,data-jpa,security)

docs.title: Common dependencies
docs.text: ID Description web Spring Web (REST API) data-jpa Spring Data JPA security Spring Security actuator Spring Boot Actuator devtools Spring Boot DevTools lombok Lombok h2 H2 Database postgresql PostgreSQL Driver

docs.title: Generated files (Maven)
docs.text:
myapp/
├── pom.xml
├── mvnw
├── mvnw.cmd
├── .mvn/
│   └── wrapper/
│       └── maven-wrapper.properties
└── src/
    ├── main/
    │   ├── java/
    │   │   └── com/example/myapp/
    │   │       └── MyappApplication.java
    │   └── resources/
    │       ├── application.properties
    │       ├── static/
    │       └── templates/
    └── test/
        └── java/
            └── com/example/myapp/
                └── MyappApplicationTests.java

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• stoke does not build or run Spring Boot projects. Use Maven or Gradle after scaffolding.
  • The Spring Initializr API returns versions with a .RELEASE suffix (e.g. 4.1.0.RELEASE), but Maven Central uses plain versions (e.g. 4.1.0). stoke strips the .RELEASE suffix automatically.

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
cd myapp
mvnw spring-boot:run        # Linux/macOS
mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run    # Windows

Or with Gradle:

gradlew bootRun             # Linux/macOS
gradlew.bat bootRun         # Windows

Default port: 8080

Open http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: Customization
docs.text: Standard Spring Boot project — refer to Spring Boot documentation for configuration.

docs.title: Chi
docs.text:
Chi 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init chi

Chi는 net/http 기반의 Go 경량 라우터입니다.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
• Project name: 프로젝트 디렉토리 이름
  • Go module name: 예: github.com/user/myapp (기본값은 프로젝트 이름)

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
myapp/
├── stoke.toml
├── go.mod
├── main.go                    # Chi 진입점
└── handlers/
    └── hello.go               # 샘플 핸들러

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: • github.com/go-chi/chi/v5 (go get으로 설치)

docs.title: 기본 설정
docs.text:
• Port: 8080
  • Middleware: Logger (기본 포함)
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → {"message": "Hello from Chi + stoke!"}
  • GET /hello/{name} → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

브라우저: http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: 커스터마이징
docs.text:
• 포트 변경: main.go의 http.ListenAndServe(":8080", r) 수정
  • 핸들러 추가: handlers/에 파일 생성, main.go에서 등록
  • 미들웨어 추가: main.go의 r.Use(...) 사용

docs.title: 참고
docs.text: Chi는 Go의 net/http 규칙을 따르므로 핸들러가 func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) 형식입니다. 기존 Go HTTP 라이브러리와 미들웨어를 그대로 사용할 수 있습니다.

docs.title: Django
docs.text:
Django 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init django

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
• Project name: 프로젝트 디렉토리 이름 (Django 프로젝트 패키지 이름으로도 사용)
  • Python version: 감지된 파이썬 목록에서 선택 (stoke init이랑 동일)
  • Environment type: venv 또는 conda

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
myapp/
├── stoke.toml
└── src/
    ├── main.py                        # stoke run 진입점 (manage.py runserver 호출)
    ├── manage.py                      # Django 관리 CLI
    ├── myapp/                         # 프로젝트 패키지
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── settings.py                # Django 설정
    │   ├── urls.py                    # 루트 URL 설정
    │   ├── wsgi.py
    │   └── asgi.py
    ├── hello/                         # 샘플 앱
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── apps.py
    │   ├── views.py
    │   ├── urls.py
    │   └── templates/
    │       └── hello/
    │           └── index.html         # 샘플 템플릿
    └── static/
        └── style.css                  # 샘플 스타일시트

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: • django (최신)

docs.title: 기본 설정
docs.text:
• Host: 127.0.0.1
  • Port: 8000
  • DEBUG: True
  • Database: SQLite (db.sqlite3)
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → HTML 페이지 (hello/templates/hello/index.html 렌더링)
  • GET /api/hello/<name>/ → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}
  • GET /admin/ → Django 관리자 페이지

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

브라우저: http://localhost:8000/

docs.title: 참고 사항
docs.text:
• main.py가 manage.py runserver를 subprocess로 호출하므로 stoke run이 개발 서버 시작
  • 초기 마이그레이션 18개 대기 중 — python src/manage.py migrate로 적용
  • 프로덕션에는 gunicorn, uvicorn 등 WSGI/ASGI 서버 사용 필요

docs.title: 커스터마이징
docs.text:
• 포트 변경: src/main.py의 runserver에 인자 전달
  • 뷰 추가: src/hello/views.py 편집, src/hello/urls.py에 등록
  • 앱 추가: src/에 새 폴더 생성, settings.py의 INSTALLED_APPS에 등록
  • 템플릿 추가: 앱의 templates/<앱이름>/에 .html 파일 생성
  • 정적 파일 추가: src/static/에 배치

docs.title: 기본 설정

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
• Project name: 프로젝트 디렉토리 이름
  • Go module name: 예: github.com/user/myapp (기본값은 프로젝트 이름)

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
myapp/
├── stoke.toml
├── go.mod
├── main.go                    # Fiber 진입점
└── handlers/
    └── hello.go               # 샘플 핸들러

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: • github.com/gofiber/fiber/v2 (go get으로 설치)

docs.title: 기본 설정
docs.text:
• Port: 3000
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → {"message": "Hello from Fiber + stoke!"}
  • GET /hello/:name → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

브라우저: http://localhost:3000/

docs.title: 커스터마이징
docs.text:
• 포트 변경: main.go의 app.Listen(":3000") 수정
  • 핸들러 추가: handlers/에 파일 생성, main.go에서 등록
  • 미들웨어 추가: main.go의 app.Use(...) 사용

docs.title: Flask
docs.text:
Flask 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init flask

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
• Project name: 프로젝트 디렉토리 이름
  • Python version: 감지된 파이썬 목록에서 선택 (stoke init이랑 동일)
  • Environment type: venv 또는 conda

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
myapp/
├── stoke.toml
└── src/
    ├── main.py                        # Flask 진입점
    └── app/
        ├── __init__.py                # create_app()
        ├── routes.py                  # register_routes()
        ├── templates/
        │   └── index.html             # Jinja2 템플릿
        └── static/
            └── style.css              # 샘플 스타일시트

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: • flask (최신)

docs.title: 기본 설정
docs.text:
• Host: 0.0.0.0
  • Port: 5000
  • Debug mode: on
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → HTML 페이지 (templates/index.html 렌더링)
  • GET /api/hello/<name> → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

브라우저: http://localhost:5000/

docs.title: 커스터마이징
docs.text:
• 포트 변경: src/main.py에서 port=5000 수정
  • 라우트 추가: src/app/routes.py 편집
  • 템플릿 추가: src/app/templates/에 .html 파일 생성
  • 정적 파일 추가: src/app/static/에 CSS/JS/이미지 배치

docs.title: Gin
docs.text:
Gin 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init gin

Gin은 Go의 인기 있는 고성능 HTTP 웹 프레임워크입니다.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
• Project name: 프로젝트 디렉토리 이름
  • Go module name: 예: github.com/user/myapp (기본값은 프로젝트 이름)

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
myapp/
├── stoke.toml
├── go.mod
├── main.go                    # Gin 진입점
└── handlers/
    └── hello.go               # 샘플 핸들러

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: • github.com/gin-gonic/gin (go get으로 설치)

docs.title: 기본 설정
docs.text:
• Port: 8080
  • Endpoints:
  • GET / → {"message": "Hello from Gin + stoke!"}
  • GET /hello/:name → {"message": "Hello, {name}!"}

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
cd myapp
stoke build
stoke run

브라우저: http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: 커스터마이징
docs.text:
• 포트 변경: main.go의 r.Run(":8080") 수정
  • 핸들러 추가: handlers/에 파일 생성, main.go에서 등록
  • 미들웨어 추가: main.go의 r.Use(...) 사용

docs.title: Hono
docs.text:
Hono 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init hono

Hono는 엣지 컴퓨팅(Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Bun, Node.js)을 위한 가볍고 빠른 웹 프레임워크입니다.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
stoke는 npm create hono@latest를 호출하며 다음을 물어봅니다:

  • Project name
  • Template: Cloudflare Workers, Cloudflare Pages, Deno, Bun, Node.js, Vercel, AWS Lambda 등
  • Package manager

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text: 템플릿(엣지 플랫폼)에 따라 다름.

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: Hono 코어 + 플랫폼별 의존성.

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
stoke가 관리하지 않음 — 플랫폼별 명령어 사용:

cd myapp
npm install
npm run dev

포트와 URL은 선택한 플랫폼에 따라 다름 (Cloudflare Workers는 보통 http://localhost:8787/).

docs.title: 참고
docs.text:
• stoke는 Hono 프로젝트를 빌드하거나 실행하지 않음
  • Hono는 엣지 런타임에 최적화됨
  • Node.js, Deno, Bun, Cloudflare Workers, Vercel, AWS Lambda 호환
  • 자세한 내용은 Hono 공식 문서 참조

docs.title: NestJS
docs.text:
NestJS 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init nestjs

NestJS는 Angular 스타일 아키텍처 (모듈, 컨트롤러, 서비스)를 사용하는 TypeScript 백엔드 프레임워크입니다.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
• Project name
  • Package manager: npm / yarn / pnpm (NestJS CLI)

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
표준 NestJS 구조:

myapp/
├── src/
│   ├── main.ts
│   ├── app.module.ts
│   ├── app.controller.ts
│   └── app.service.ts
├── test/
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
└── nest-cli.json

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: 표준 NestJS 의존성 (@nestjs/common, @nestjs/core, rxjs 등).

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
stoke가 관리하지 않음 — NestJS 명령어 직접 사용:

cd myapp
npm run start:dev     # 개발 (핫 리로드)
npm run build         # 프로덕션 빌드
npm run start:prod    # 프로덕션 서버

브라우저: http://localhost:3000/

docs.title: 참고
docs.text:
• stoke는 NestJS 프로젝트를 빌드하거나 실행하지 않음
  • 모듈 추가: nest g module <name>
  • 컨트롤러 추가: nest g controller <name>
  • 자세한 내용은 NestJS 공식 문서 참조

docs.title: Next.js
docs.text:
Next.js 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init nextjs

Next.js는 SSR, SSG, 파일 기반 라우팅을 지원하는 React 풀스택 프레임워크입니다.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
stoke는 create-next-app을 호출하며 다음을 물어봅니다:

  • Project name
  • TypeScript?
  • ESLint?
  • Tailwind CSS?
  • src/ directory?
  • App Router?
  • Turbopack?
  • Import alias?

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text: 표준 Next.js 구조 (옵션에 따라 다름). package.json, next.config.ts, app/ 또는 pages/ 등.

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: 표준 Next.js 의존성 (Next.js, React, TypeScript 선택 시).

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
stoke가 관리하지 않음 — Next.js 명령어 직접 사용:

cd myapp
npm run dev         # 개발 서버
npm run build       # 프로덕션 빌드
npm start           # 프로덕션 서버

브라우저: http://localhost:3000/

docs.title: 참고
docs.text:
• stoke는 Next.js 프로젝트를 빌드하거나 실행하지 않음
  • Next.js는 React 애플리케이션에 최적화된 자체 빌드 시스템 사용
  • 자세한 내용은 Next.js 공식 문서 참조

docs.title: Nuxt
docs.text:
Nuxt 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init nuxt

Nuxt는 SSR, SSG, 파일 기반 라우팅을 지원하는 Vue.js 풀스택 프레임워크입니다.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
stoke는 nuxi init을 호출하며 다음을 물어봅니다:

  • Template: minimal 등
  • Package manager: npm / yarn / pnpm / bun
  • Initialize git repository?

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
표준 Nuxt 구조:

myapp/
├── app.vue
├── nuxt.config.ts
├── package.json
└── (pages/, components/, etc.)

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: 표준 Nuxt 의존성 (nuxt, vue).

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
stoke가 관리하지 않음 — Nuxt 명령어 직접 사용:

cd myapp
npm install
npm run dev         # 개발 서버
npm run build       # 프로덕션 빌드
npm run preview     # 프로덕션 미리보기

브라우저: http://localhost:3000/

docs.title: 참고
docs.text:
• stoke는 Nuxt 프로젝트를 빌드하거나 실행하지 않음
  • Nuxt는 Next.js와 비슷한 파일 기반 라우팅을 Vue에서 사용
  • 자세한 내용은 Nuxt 공식 문서 참조

docs.title: 프레임워크 스캐폴딩
docs.text:
stoke는 인기 프레임워크의 실행 가능한 프로젝트를 명령어 하나로 생성합니다:

# Java
stoke init spring-boot

# Python
stoke init fastapi
stoke init flask
stoke init django

# Go
stoke init gin
stoke init echo
stoke init fiber
stoke init chi

# JavaScript
stoke init express
stoke init fastify

# TypeScript
stoke init nextjs
stoke init nestjs
stoke init vite
stoke init nuxt
stoke init sveltekit
stoke init hono

각 명령어는 기본 설정을 대화형으로 받고 샘플 코드와 함께 프로젝트 구조를 생성합니다.

docs.title: Java
docs.text: 명령어 프레임워크 stoke init spring-boot Spring Boot (Spring Initializr 사용)

docs.title: Python
docs.text: 명령어 프레임워크 stoke init fastapi FastAPI + uvicorn stoke init flask Flask + Jinja2 템플릿 stoke init django Django (프로젝트 + 앱 전체 구조)

docs.title: Go
docs.text: 명령어 프레임워크 stoke init gin Gin — 인기 있는 고성능 HTTP 프레임워크 stoke init echo Echo — 미니멀, 고성능 stoke init fiber Fiber — Express 스타일 API stoke init chi Chi — 표준 라이브러리 기반 경량 라우터

docs.title: JavaScript
docs.text: 명령어 프레임워크 stoke init express Express — 클래식 Node.js 웹 프레임워크 stoke init fastify Fastify — 빠르고 오버헤드 적음

docs.title: TypeScript
docs.text: 명령어 프레임워크 stoke init nextjs Next.js — React 풀스택 stoke init nestjs NestJS — Angular 스타일 백엔드 stoke init vite Vite — 빠른 프론트엔드 빌드 도구 stoke init nuxt Nuxt — Vue 풀스택 stoke init sveltekit SvelteKit — Svelte 풀스택 stoke init hono Hono — 엣지 컴퓨팅 프레임워크

docs.title: 생성되는 것
docs.text:
각 프레임워크별 페이지에서 확인:

Java: - Spring Boot

Python: - FastAPI - Flask - Django

Go: - Gin - Echo - Fiber - Chi

JavaScript: - Express - Fastify

TypeScript: - Next.js - NestJS - Vite - Nuxt - SvelteKit - Hono

docs.title: 스캐폴딩 후
docs.text:
Python, Go, JavaScript 프레임워크 (Express, Fastify)는 동일한 워크플로우:

stoke build
stoke run

Spring Boot는 Maven 사용:

mvnw spring-boot:run      # Linux/macOS
mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run  # Windows

TypeScript 프레임워크 (Next.js, NestJS, Vite, Nuxt, SvelteKit, Hono)는 각 프레임워크 명령어 사용:

npm run dev     # 개발
npm run build   # 프로덕션

stoke는 공식 스캐폴딩 도구 (create-next-app, @nestjs/cli, nuxi, sv create 등)를 호출하므로 최신 프로젝트 템플릿을 얻습니다.

docs.title: Spring Boot
docs.text:
Spring Boot 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init spring-boot

Spring Initializr API를 사용해서 표준 Spring Boot 프로젝트 다운로드.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
• Project name: 프로젝트 디렉토리 이름
  • Group ID: Maven groupId (예: com.example)
  • Spring Boot version: 사용 가능한 RELEASE 버전 중 선택 (Spring Initializr에서 조회)
  • Java version: 17, 21, 25
  • Build tool: Maven 또는 Gradle
  • Packaging: jar 또는 war
  • Dependencies: 콤마로 구분 (예: web,data-jpa,security)

docs.title: 자주 사용하는 의존성
docs.text: ID 설명 web Spring Web (REST API) data-jpa Spring Data JPA security Spring Security actuator Spring Boot Actuator devtools Spring Boot DevTools lombok Lombok h2 H2 Database postgresql PostgreSQL Driver

docs.title: 생성 파일 (Maven)
docs.text:
myapp/
├── pom.xml
├── mvnw
├── mvnw.cmd
├── .mvn/
│   └── wrapper/
│       └── maven-wrapper.properties
└── src/
    ├── main/
    │   ├── java/
    │   │   └── com/example/myapp/
    │   │       └── MyappApplication.java
    │   └── resources/
    │       ├── application.properties
    │       ├── static/
    │       └── templates/
    └── test/
        └── java/
            └── com/example/myapp/
                └── MyappApplicationTests.java

docs.title: 참고 사항
docs.text:
• stoke는 Spring Boot 프로젝트를 빌드하거나 실행하지 않음. 스캐폴딩 후 Maven 또는 Gradle 사용.
  • Spring Initializr API는 .RELEASE 접미사가 붙은 버전 반환 (예: 4.1.0.RELEASE). Maven Central은 접미사 없는 버전만 사용 (예: 4.1.0). stoke가 .RELEASE 접미사를 자동으로 제거.

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
cd myapp
mvnw spring-boot:run        # Linux/macOS
mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run    # Windows

Gradle 사용 시:

gradlew bootRun             # Linux/macOS
gradlew.bat bootRun         # Windows

기본 포트: 8080

브라우저: http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: 커스터마이징
docs.text: 표준 Spring Boot 프로젝트 — 설정은 Spring Boot 공식 문서 참조.

docs.title: SvelteKit
docs.text:
SvelteKit 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init sveltekit

SvelteKit은 SSR, SSG, 파일 기반 라우팅을 지원하는 Svelte 풀스택 프레임워크입니다.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
stoke는 npx sv create를 호출하며 다음을 물어봅니다:

  • Template: SvelteKit minimal, demo app, library
  • TypeScript?
  • Additional tools: ESLint, Prettier, Playwright, Vitest

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text:
표준 SvelteKit 구조:

myapp/
├── src/
│   ├── routes/
│   └── app.html
├── static/
├── svelte.config.js
├── vite.config.ts
└── package.json

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: 표준 SvelteKit 의존성.

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
stoke가 관리하지 않음 — SvelteKit 명령어 직접 사용:

cd myapp
npm install
npm run dev         # 개발 서버
npm run build       # 프로덕션 빌드
npm run preview     # 프로덕션 미리보기

브라우저: http://localhost:5173/

docs.title: 참고
docs.text:
• stoke는 SvelteKit 프로젝트를 빌드하거나 실행하지 않음
  • SvelteKit은 빌드 시 Svelte 컴포넌트를 컴파일
  • 자세한 내용은 SvelteKit 공식 문서 참조

docs.title: Vite
docs.text:
Vite 프로젝트 생성:

stoke init vite

Vite는 빠른 프론트엔드 빌드 도구입니다. React, Vue, Svelte, 순수 JavaScript/TypeScript 지원.

docs.title: 프롬프트
docs.text:
stoke는 npm create vite@latest를 호출하며 다음을 물어봅니다:

  • Project name
  • Framework: React, Vue, Svelte, Solid, Preact, Lit, Qwik, Angular, vanilla
  • Variant: JavaScript / TypeScript

docs.title: 생성 파일
docs.text: 표준 Vite 프로젝트 구조 (프레임워크 선택에 따라 다름).

docs.title: 의존성
docs.text: 프레임워크별 의존성 + Vite 빌드 도구.

docs.title: 실행
docs.text:
stoke가 관리하지 않음 — Vite 명령어 직접 사용:

cd myapp
npm install
npm run dev         # 개발 서버
npm run build       # 프로덕션 빌드
npm run preview     # 프로덕션 미리보기

브라우저: Vite가 표시하는 URL (보통 http://localhost:5173/)

docs.title: 참고
docs.text:
• stoke는 Vite 프로젝트를 빌드하거나 실행하지 않음
  • Vite는 빠른 개발을 위한 HMR (Hot Module Replacement) 지원
  • 자세한 내용은 Vite 공식 문서 참조

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: stoke is available for Windows, Linux, and macOS. Choose your platform:

docs.title: Option 1: Windows installer (recommended)
docs.text:
Download the latest installer from GitHub Releases:

  • File: stoke-setup-X.Y.Z.exe
  • Install location: %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\stoke
  • No prerequisites — Python is bundled with the installer
  • No admin privileges required
  • Option to add to PATH during installation

After installation, verify:

stoke --version

docs.title: Option 2: pip
docs.text:
If you already have Python 3.10+ installed:

pip install stoke-build

docs.title: Linux / macOS
docs.text:
Install via pip:

pip install stoke-build

Requires Python 3.10 or higher.

docs.title: Verify installation
docs.text:
stoke --version

Should output the installed version.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
stoke itself: - Windows installer: no prerequisites (Python is bundled) - pip install: Python 3.10 or higher required

Language toolchains (auto-installable via stoke install --language=X): - Python projects: Python 3.8+ (any version stoke can detect) - Java projects: JDK 17 or higher (Adoptium/OpenJDK/Zulu recommended) - C/C++ projects: gcc, g++, or clang - C/C++ libraries: vcpkg (auto-installable via stoke install vcpkg)

docs.title: Checking installed toolchains
docs.text:
After installation, check what languages stoke can build:

stoke python list      # Show detected Python installations
stoke java list        # Show detected JDKs
stoke c list           # Show detected C compilers
stoke cpp list         # Show detected C++ compilers

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start — build your first project
  • Configuration — stoke.toml reference

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks you through creating and building your first stoke project.

docs.title: Create a new project
docs.text:
Use stoke init to scaffold a new project interactively:

mkdir myapp
cd myapp
stoke init

The wizard will ask:

  • Project name
  • Language (Python, Java, C, C++)
  • Version requirements
  • Dependencies

It generates:

  • stoke.toml — project configuration
  • src/ — source directory with a hello-world file

docs.title: Build
docs.text:
stoke build

Output: Building 'myapp' (cpp)... Using cpp compiler 15.2.0 Compiled 1 file(s) Build complete: myapp

Build artifacts land in .stoke/.

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
stoke run

Output: Running: ...\myapp.exe Hello from stoke!

docs.title: Watch mode
docs.text:
Auto-rebuild on file changes:

stoke watch

Press Ctrl+C to stop.

docs.title: Hot-reload
docs.text:
Rebuild and restart the process automatically:

stoke hot-reload

Useful for servers or long-running processes.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "python"
python_version = "3.12"
entry = "src/main.py"

[targets.myapp.deps]
requests = "*"

stoke build     # Creates venv, installs deps
stoke run       # Runs src/main.py

docs.title: Java
docs.text:
[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "java"
java_version = "25"
sources = ["src/main/java/**/*.java"]
main_class = "com.example.Main"

stoke build     # Compiles .java files
stoke run       # Runs main_class

docs.title: C
docs.text:
[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "c"
sources = ["src/**/*.c"]

stoke build     # Compiles + links
stoke run       # Runs the executable

docs.title: C++
docs.text:
[project]
name = "myapp"
version = "0.1.0"

[targets.myapp]
language = "cpp"
sources = ["src/**/*.cpp"]

stoke build
stoke run
